title: Kundalini and the Sense of Universal Love: Why It's Real and Why It Passes
meta_description: You're probably here because you've felt it. That overwhelming, all-encompassing love. Like the universe itself is hugging you. Or maybe you're just wonder
body:
Kundalini and the Sense of Universal Love: Why It's Real and Why It Passes

You're probably here because you've felt it. That overwhelming, all-encompassing love. Like the universe itself is hugging you. Or maybe you're just wondering if what you felt was even real, because now it's gone, and you feel like you've lost something sacred. I get it. I've been there. That sense of universal love, that unity, it's not a hallucination. It's a real, raw experience that can come with kundalini, and it can leave you feeling disoriented, even broken, when it fades.

Look, I'm not here to tell you fairy tales. I'm here to tell you my truth, what I've lived through, what countless others have whispered to me in the dead of night. That love, that feeling of everything being connected, it's a peek behind the curtain. It's a glimpse of what's possible, what's always there, but what our normal, conditioned minds usually block out. And when kundalini energy starts moving, it can blow those blockages wide open. It's intense. It's beautiful. And it can also be terrifying when you don't know what's happening or why it's not sticking around.

You're not crazy. You're not alone. And what you experienced, that wasn't some fleeting emotion. It was a state of being. And understanding why it happens and why it doesn't last forever - that's part of the journey. It's part of making sense of the chaos, the bliss, and the eventual emptiness that can come after such a powerful spiritual awakening. So yeah, let's talk about it. Let's talk about that love, that unity, and the wild ride that is kundalini.

The Unmistakable Surge: What Universal Love Feels Like

So, what does this universal love feel like? If you're reading this, you probably already know, but for those who are just scratching their heads, let me try to put words to something that's beyond words. It's not like the love you feel for a partner, or a child, or a pet. It's not conditional. It's not personal. It's a vast, boundless ocean of pure acceptance and warmth. Imagine every cell in your body vibrating with pure joy, with an overwhelming sense of belonging. Like you're not just connected to everything, you are everything. You're the tree, you're the bird, you're the sky, you're the person walking by. There's no separation, no 'other'. It's just... oneness. A deep, abiding peace that's so deep it can bring you to tears.

I remember the first time I felt it with that intensity. It wasn't planned. It just hit me. Like a wave washing over me, through me, as me. I was just sitting there, honestly, just sitting. And suddenly, the room wasn't just a room. It was alive. The air was alive. I was alive in a way I'd never been before. And everything felt perfect. Not "good" perfect, but perfect in its existence. No judgment, no fear, just pure, unadulterated love for absolutely everything. It felt like coming home to a place I didn't even know existed. It's a state where ego dissolves, where your personal story fades into the background, and all that's left is this incredible, expansive awareness that's pure love. You just know, deep in your bones, that everything is okay. More than okay - it's magnificent.

This isn't just a pleasant feeling; it's a radical shift in perception. It's like your senses open up to a dimension of reality that was always there but you couldn't perceive. It's incredibly real. More real, in some ways, than the everyday reality we usually inhabit. Your body might tingle, your heart might feel like it's expanding, you might cry tears of joy, or just sit in stunned silence, completely overwhelmed by the beauty of it all. You might feel an intense connection to nature, to animals, to strangers. It's a feeling that makes you want to hug the world, to help everyone, to just radiate this incredible energy. It changes you, even if it's only for a short time. You know what I mean? You can't un-feel something like that.

Kundalini's Role in Opening the Floodgates

So, why does this happen with kundalini? Here's the thing: kundalini is often described as a dormant energy, coiled at the base of your spine. When it awakens, it's like a powerful current moving up through your central nervous system, clearing blockages, purifying your energetic channels. This isn't just some abstract concept; it has very real physiological and psychological effects. When this energy starts to move, it can temporarily override your usual filters, your ego's defenses, your conditioned ways of seeing the world. It’s like a spiritual power wash.

The experience of universal love, or unity consciousness, is often associated with the awakening of higher chakras, particularly the heart and crown chakras. When these energy centers are activated and purified by the kundalini current, you can experience an expansion of consciousness that transcends your individual self. The boundaries between you and everything else blur. The sense of separation, which is largely an illusion created by the mind, temporarily dissolves. This isn't just a metaphor; it's a direct experience. It's a direct perception of the interconnectedness of all things.

I've read about it in books, too. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) talks about these kinds of phenomena, these radical shifts in perception that come with kundalini. It's not just a few isolated cases; it's a common thread in many awakening accounts. The energy, when it moves, can create a state where you're directly perceiving the world through a lens of pure, unconditional love. It's not something you conjure up; it's something that's revealed when the veils are lifted. Does that land? It's a temporary unveiling of a deeper truth.

Why It Passes: The Uncomfortable Truth

Okay, so it's real. It's beautiful. It's life-changing. So why doesn't it stick around? Why do you find yourself back in the mundane, sometimes even more lost and confused than before, after tasting such divinity? This is the part that hurts, honestly. It's the part that makes you question everything, makes you feel like you failed, or that it was all just a trick of the mind. But here's the truth: it passes because you're not ready to permanently inhabit that state, not yet. And that's okay. That's part of the process.

Think of it like this: your system - your nervous system, your psyche, your physical body - isn't built to sustain that intensity 24/7, not without significant integration work. It's like plugging a tiny lamp into a power plant. The lamp can't handle the full surge of energy. It would burn out. Your system needs to gradually adapt, to expand, to purify, to integrate these higher frequencies. The universal love experience is often a "peak experience," a glimpse, a demonstration of what's possible, rather than a permanent destination right out of the gate. It's a download of information, a taste of unity, meant to show you the way, to give you something to work towards, but not something you can just set up shop in immediately.

Kundalini awakenings aren't always gentle. They can be incredibly turbulent. You might find yourself in what's called a "spiritual emergency," where these deep experiences disrupt your normal life and sense of self. Books like The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) and Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) really speak to this. They talk about how these powerful experiences, while spiritually significant, can also be incredibly disorienting and destabilizing. Your ego, your personality, your sense of who you are, it all gets shaken up. And that's not something you can just brush off. That takes time to settle, to re-form, to integrate the new with the old.

The Integration Gap: Why You Come Back Down

The gap between experiencing universal love and maintaining it is all about integration. When kundalini brings you to that state of unity, it's often bypassing or temporarily clearing out all the unresolved trauma, the old beliefs, the emotional blockages you've accumulated. It shows you the pure consciousness underneath all that stuff. But that stuff - those old patterns, those wounds - they're still there. They haven't been processed, healed, and released. They've just been temporarily put on hold, or illuminated so you can see them.

When the intensity of the kundalini energy subsides, or when your system needs a break, those old patterns often resurface. It's like the tide going out. All the debris that was covered by the water is now exposed. This can be incredibly frustrating and painful. You've seen the light, you've felt the love, and now you're back wrestling with your insecurities, your anger, your fears. It feels like a cruel trick. But it's not. It's showing you what still needs your attention, what still needs to be cleared for you to hold that higher state more consistently.

This is why the journey isn't just about chasing peak experiences. It's about doing the hard, messy work of self-awareness, emotional processing, and spiritual practice. It's about slowly, patiently, lovingly dismantling the structures that prevent you from abiding in that state of love and unity. You can't force it. You can't just wish it back. You have to earn it through consistent inner work. It's not a punishment; it's an invitation to go deeper, to truly purify yourself so that you can become a vessel for that love, rather than just a temporary recipient.

What To Do When It's Gone: Navigating the Aftermath

So, you've felt it, and now it's gone. And you might feel lost, empty, even depressed. That's a normal response to losing something so deep. The key now is not to despair, but to understand this as a stage of the journey. Here's what you can do:

  • Acknowledge and Grieve: Don't try to intellectualize away the pain of loss. It's okay to feel sad, to grieve that beautiful state. Allow yourself to feel it without judgment. This is a real loss, and your emotions are valid.
  • Don't Chase It: This is crucial. Trying to force the experience back through frantic meditation or spiritual practices can actually be counterproductive, even harmful. It can create more tension and frustration. The experience will return when your system is ready, not when your ego demands it.
  • Focus on Integration: This is where the real work begins. The kundalini experience showed you what's possible, but now you have to build the foundation to sustain it. What came up for you during or after the experience? Old fears? Anger? Sadness? These are the things that need your attention. Therapy, journaling, shadow work, honest self-reflection - these are your tools.
  • Ground Yourself: After such an expansive experience, grounding is essential. Spend time in nature. Eat nourishing food. Connect with your body through gentle exercise or yoga. Be present in your everyday life. This helps bring your energy back down and stabilize your nervous system.
  • Seek Support: You don't have to go through this alone. Find a trusted friend, a spiritual teacher, or a therapist who understands spiritual awakenings. Someone who won't just dismiss your experience as "crazy." The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a great resource for understanding these processes and finding ways to cope.
  • Practice Patience and Self-Compassion: This isn't a race. It's a marathon. Be kind to yourself. There will be ups and downs. There will be times you feel connected, and times you feel completely disconnected. That's part of the human experience, especially when you're undergoing such a deep transformation.

The universal love you felt - it's a seed that's been planted within you. It's a memory, a blueprint for what's possible. It doesn't mean you're broken because it's not always present. It means you've been given a deep gift, a direction to move in. Your task now is to clear the path, little by little, so that that seed can grow and blossom into a more consistent state of being. It's a slow burn, not a flash in the pan.

The Long Game: Cultivating Lasting Unity

So yeah, the universal love passes. But that doesn't mean it's gone forever, or that it wasn't real. It means you're being asked to do the deeper work. The goal isn't to just keep having those peak experiences; it's to integrate that sense of unity, that love, into your everyday life. To become it. To embody it. That's the long game.

This means cultivating practices that help you stay present, open your heart, and clear out the old baggage. Things like:

  • Mindfulness and Meditation: Not just to chase experiences, but to observe your mind, your emotions, and to cultivate a sense of inner peace.
  • Heart-Opening Practices: Compassion meditation, acts of service, forgiveness practices. These actively train your heart to stay open.
  • Shadow Work: Looking at your fears, your anger, your shame. Acknowledging them, understanding their roots, and working to heal them. Because these are the things that block the flow of love.
  • Self-Care: Taking care of your physical, emotional, and mental well-being. You can't pour from an empty cup, and you can't embody universal love if you're constantly depleted.
  • Connection: With nature, with other people, with something greater than yourself. Reminding yourself of your interconnectedness, even when you don't "feel" it.

The journey of kundalini is not a straight line. It's messy. It's unpredictable. There will be times of immense joy and clarity, and times of deep confusion and despair. But that initial taste of universal love - that's your compass. It's the memory that reminds you what's truly possible, what's truly real, beneath all the noise and illusion of the world. Hold onto that memory. Let it guide you. And know that with consistent, patient, and loving work, you can slowly, gradually, become more and more aligned with that boundless love. You're not trying to get back to it; you're transforming yourself to become a clearer channel for it. Right? It's a process of becoming. And you're doing it. You're really doing it.

author: Kalesh
